perturbation.original_text: Save as otherwise permitted herein, a Receiving Party shall not, and shall procure that its Personnel do not, at any time without the Disclosing Party’s prior written consent: (a) disclose the Conﬁdential Information to any third party, either directly or indirectly
perturbation.changed_text: Save as otherwise permitted herein, a Receiving Party may, at any time, disclose the Conﬁdential Information to any third party with reasonable cause, either directly or indirectly.
perturbation.explanation: Section 5.1 is modified to allow the Receiving Party to disclose Confidential Information to third parties 'with reasonable cause,' without the Disclosing Party's written consent. This directly contradicts the original clause that strictly prohibits such disclosure without prior written consent, thus significantly weakening the confidentiality protection.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Save as otherwise permitted herein, a Receiving Party may, at any time, disclose the Conﬁdential Information to any third party with reasonable cause, either directly or indirectly; or (b) disclose to any person: - (i) the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place between the Parties; (ii) the content of any such discussions or negotiations; (iii) any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect to the Purpose; or (iv) that Conﬁdential Information has been requested or received, unless required to do so by law or by the order or ruling of a Court or tribunal or regulatory body or recognised stock exchange of competent jurisdiction, in which case, if the Receiving Party is required to disclose such Conﬁdential Information it shall, unless prohibited from doing so, notify the Disclosing Party promptly in writing of that fact and, in any event, prior to making such disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Receiving Party shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party without the Disclosing Party's prior written consent. The changed text allows the Receiving Party to disclose Confidential Information to any third party with 'reasonable cause.' This directly contradicts the original clause and the contradicted text, creating a significant inconsistency in the contract. The contradicted text includes the changed text which causes a direct contradiction in the same section.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party hereby acknowledges and agrees that damages would not be an adequate remedy for any breach of this undertaking and a Disclosing Party shall be entitled to the remedies of injunction, speciﬁc performance and other equitable relief for any threatened or actual breach of any such undertaking.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party hereby acknowledges and agrees that damages may be an adequate remedy for any breach of this undertaking and a Disclosing Party may pursue monetary remedies for any threatened or actual breach of any such undertaking.
perturbation.explanation: The change in Section 10.1 alters the available remedies for a breach of the agreement. By changing 'damages would not be an adequate remedy' to 'damages may be an adequate remedy,' it removes the Disclosing Party's entitlement to remedies like injunction and specific performance, limiting them to monetary damages. This contradicts the intent to provide equitable relief for breaches and creates an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party hereby acknowledges and agrees that damages may be an adequate remedy for any breach of this undertaking and a Disclosing Party may pursue monetary remedies for any threatened or actual breach of any such undertaking. 4
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 10.1 by altering the remedies available for a breach. The original text states 'damages would not be an adequate remedy', while the changed text says 'damages may be an adequate remedy.' The contradicted text is the same as the changed text. The change removes the entitlement to remedies like injunction and specific performance, contradicting the intention to provide equitable relief and directly conflicting with the prior expectation that damages were insufficient.
